introduction.p: Optimization chose best points. Integration asks a different question:
introduction.md: \text{How much is spread over the whole region?}
introduction.p: That region may be a plate, a room, a pond, a solid object, or a distorted image. The same idea keeps returning. Cut the region into small pieces. Estimate the contribution from each piece. Add.
These projects turn that sentence into measurements.

subsection.title: Project 1: estimate mass from measured density data
subsection.p: A formula is not always available. Sometimes the density is known only from measurements.

A lab technician may test a metal plate at several points. A geologist may sample soil density at grid locations. A medical scan may give density values one voxel at a time. The integral is still the right idea, but the integral has to be estimated from data.
A seed example: a nonuniform plate. A thin rectangular plate occupies
where x and y are measured in centimeters.
The plate is divided into 3 columns and 2 rows. Each small rectangle has width 2 and height 2, so
The density, measured in grams per square centimeter, is sampled at the center of each small rectangle:
The mass is
Using the midpoint rule, we estimate
So the estimated mass is
The area of the plate is
So the estimated average density is

figure.caption: Measured density data can be turned into a mass estimate by a Riemann sum.

subsection.p: Your task. Choose one of the following.
Option A. Plate from tabulated data. Use the density table from the seed example, or create your own 4\times4 or 5\times5 grid of density values. Estimate the mass of the plate.

Option B. Temperature or pollution on a map. Use a rectangular map region. Let the data values represent temperature, pollution concentration, or rainfall. Estimate the total amount and the average value over the region.
Option C. Three-dimensional density data. A box is divided into small rectangular boxes. At the center of each box, a density value is measured. Estimate
For a simple version, use a 2\times2\times2 grid.

li.p: Draw the region and the sampling grid.
State the units of the density.
State the area or volume of each small piece:
Write the Riemann sum:
or
Estimate the total mass or total amount.
Estimate the average density:
Discuss whether the estimate should improve with a finer grid.
Which parts of the region have the largest density?
Does the data suggest a smooth density field or a sharp jump?
If one measurement were wrong, which one would most affect the estimate?
What changes if the data are sampled at corners instead of centers?
How would your estimate change if the grid cells had unequal sizes?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: the data table,
a grid drawing,
the Riemann sum,
the estimated total amount,
the estimated average value,
one paragraph explaining the main source of error.

subsection.p: The main point is that an integral can survive without a formula. A formula is one way to know a field. Measurements are another.

subsection.title: Project 2: moments of inertia of real objects
subsection.p: Mass tells how much material an object has. Moment of inertia tells how that mass is spread around an axis.

A heavy object is harder to spin if more of its mass is far from the axis. That is why a figure skater spins faster when pulling in her arms. The mass has not changed, but the mass has moved closer to the rotation axis.
The integral measures this spreading.
For a thin plate with density \delta(x,y), this becomes
A seed example: a rectangular tray. A rectangular tray is 4 meters long and 2 meters wide. Put its center at the origin, so the tray occupies
Suppose the tray has constant areal density
Find the moment of inertia about the vertical axis through its center.
The distance from the point (x,y) to the vertical axis is
So
Thus
Separate the integral:
So
The tray's mass is
The radius of gyration is the distance R_g that would give the same moment of inertia if all the mass were concentrated on a circle:
Thus
This number is not the physical radius of the tray. It is a weighted average distance from the axis.

figure.caption: Mass farther from the axis contributes more to moment of inertia.

subsection.p: Your task. Choose one object or model.
Option A. Uniform rectangular plate. Compute the moment of inertia of a rectangular plate about one of its symmetry axes and about the axis perpendicular to the plate through its center.
Option B. Disk or annulus. Use polar coordinates to compute the moment of inertia of a uniform disk or annulus about its central axis.
Option C. Triangular lamina. Choose a triangular region in the plane. Compute its mass and moment of inertia using double integrals.
Option D. Measured object. Approximate a real flat object by dividing it into small pieces. Estimate each piece's mass and distance from the chosen axis. Use the discrete approximation
Option E. Solid object. Compute or estimate the moment of inertia of a solid cylinder, rectangular box, or ball using a triple integral.

li.p: Draw the object and the rotation axis.
State whether the density is constant or variable.
Write the distance squared to the axis.
Set up the integral:
or
Choose coordinates that make the integral simpler.
Compute the mass.
Compute the moment of inertia.
Interpret the answer physically.
Which parts of the object contribute most to I?
What happens if the same mass is moved farther from the axis?
Does symmetry simplify the integral?
Can you verify your answer using the perpendicular-axis theorem or the parallel-axis theorem, where appropriate?
How would the answer change if the density were larger near the edge?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: a labeled diagram,
the density model,
the chosen axis,
the moment-of-inertia integral,
the calculation,
the final value with units,
a physical explanation of why the answer is reasonable.

subsection.p: Moment of inertia is an integral with a memory of distance. A mass piece does not only count by how much mass it has. It counts by where it sits.

subsection.title: Project 3: change of variables in image warping
subsection.p: A change of variables is a controlled distortion.

In , a coordinate map sent tiny squares in one coordinate plane to tiny parallelograms in another. The determinant measured how the area changed. The same idea appears in image warping. A square image may be stretched, sheared, wrapped, or bent. If we want to compute area, brightness, or ink after the warp, the Jacobian must be included.
A seed example: a square stretched into a trapezoid. Start with the unit square
Define a map
When u=0, the vertical height is 1. When u=1, the vertical height is 2. So the square is warped into a trapezoid.
The tangent vectors are
The Jacobian determinant is
subsection.md: \begin{aligned} J(u,v) \amp= \det \begin{pmatrix} x_u \amp x_v\\ y_u \amp y_v \end{pmatrix} \\ \amp= \det \begin{pmatrix} 1\amp0\\ v\amp1+u \end{pmatrix}\\ \amp= 1+u. \end{aligned}
subsection.p: So a tiny square of area du\,dv becomes a tiny region of area approximately
The area of the warped image is
That matches the ordinary trapezoid formula:
Now suppose the brightness of the warped image is
Then the total brightness is
Using the change of variables,
Compute the inner integral:
Then
So

figure.caption: A coordinate warp changes the area of small grid cells. The Jacobian records the local area scale.

subsection.p: Your task. Choose one map T from the list or create your own.
subsection.md: \begin{array}{c|c} \text{Name} \amp T(u,v)=(x,y) \\ \hline \text{shear} \amp (u+av,\ v)\\[1mm] \text{stretch} \amp (au,\ bv)\\[1mm] \text{trapezoid warp} \amp (u,\ (1+u)v)\\[1mm] \text{fold warning} \amp (u^2,\ v)\\[1mm] \text{polar annulus} \amp ((1+r)\cos\theta,\ (1+r)\sin\theta) \end{array}
subsection.p: For the polar annulus, use

li.p: Draw the parameter region.
Draw or plot the warped image.
Compute the tangent vectors:
Compute the Jacobian determinant:
Decide whether the map preserves orientation, reverses orientation, or changes sign somewhere.
Compute the area of the image:
Choose a simple brightness function B(x,y), such as
and compute
using the pullback:
Where does the map stretch area the most?
Where does it shrink area?
Does the map ever collapse a two-dimensional region into a curve?
Does the sign of J matter for ordinary area?
Where would the sign of J matter for an oriented integral such as

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: the coordinate map,
a picture of the parameter grid and the warped grid,
the Jacobian calculation,
the area calculation,
one pulled-back integral,
a paragraph explaining what the Jacobian means geometrically.

subsection.p: A warped image is a friendly place to see the change-of-variables formula. The picture changes shape, but the integral stays honest because the Jacobian keeps track of the stretching.

subsection.title: Project 4: Monte Carlo integration in two and three dimensions
subsection.p: Some regions are too irregular for clean limits. Some functions are known only through simulation. Some dimensions are too high for ordinary grid methods to be practical.
Monte Carlo integration uses random samples instead of a regular grid.
The idea is simple: average many random values, then multiply by the size of the sampling box.
A seed example: estimating water volume in a circular pond. A shallow circular pond has radius 2. Its depth is modeled by
inside the disk
The volume of water is
We could use polar coordinates:
So the exact volume is approximately
Now estimate the same integral by Monte Carlo sampling. Put the pond inside the square
The square has area 16.
Define
Then
Suppose five random points in B give the following values:
The sample average is
So the Monte Carlo estimate is
That is rough, because N=5 is tiny. With hundreds or thousands of samples, the estimate should settle closer to 12\pi.
The general Monte Carlo formula. Let E be a region inside a box B. Define
If
are random points chosen uniformly in B, then
Here \operatorname{size}(B) means area in two dimensions and volume in three dimensions.
Your task. Choose one integral.
Option A. Area of an irregular region. Estimate the area of a region E by taking
Option B. Volume under a surface. Estimate
for a region E inside a rectangle.
Option C. Mass inside a solid. Estimate
for a solid E inside a box.

Option D. Compare Monte Carlo with an exact answer. Choose an integral that can also be computed exactly, such as a disk, ball, cylinder, or box. Compare the Monte Carlo estimate with the exact value.

li.p: State the region E.
Choose a sampling box B.
Define the function g on the whole box.
Generate random sample points.
Compute the estimate for several values of N, such as
Plot or tabulate the estimates as N grows.
Compare with an exact or high-resolution numerical answer, if available.
Does the estimate appear to converge?
How much does the answer change when you run the experiment again?
What happens if the sampling box is much larger than the region E?
Why are points outside E assigned value 0?
What advantage does random sampling have in three dimensions?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: the integral being estimated,
the sampling box,
the Monte Carlo formula used,
a table of estimates for increasing N,
a plot of the estimates, if possible,
a comparison with an exact or more accurate value,
a paragraph about reliability and error.

conclusion.p: The integration projects mostly add scalar quantities: density, brightness, mass, volume, and moment. Vector calculus adds direction. A force pushes along a path. A fluid crosses a surface. A boundary can reveal an area, and a flux balance can reveal a source. The next projects return to the book's boundary theme.
